rules: CONF-1 (Confined type formation). A struct or enum may be declared `confined` with region parameters and, when it can hold a loan, one brand parameter. Only confined types may contain borrow fields (`&'r T`, `&uniq 'r T`) or fields of confined type; every region and brand parameter used by a field must appear in the declaring type's parameter list. Each confined type declares its loan kind, `confined(uniq)` or `confined(shr)`, fixed at declaration. Non-confined types remain borrow-free (existing STOR rules unchanged), so confined values are the only aggregates that carry borrows, and the checker need look nowhere else for stored interior access.

CONF-2 (Stack confinement). A confined value may only be: let-bound (or rebind-bound per LOAN-4); passed as a call argument by `own` or by a minted borrow; returned subject to LOAN-8; destructured by match subject to LOAN-5; or moved into a field of another confined value. It must never be stored into heap data, any sealed form, a non-confined aggregate, or a global, nor assigned through any reference into such a place. The statement attempting such a store is rejected at that statement.

CONF-3 (Affinity, minting, and projection). A confined binding is affine: passing it by `own`, destructuring it, or its scope end each consume it, and any later use of a consumed binding is rejected. Like an owner binding, a live confined binding may mint one fresh borrow (`&h` or `&uniq h`) per statement; the minted borrow is itself consumed by the receiving call (T-A unchanged), so minting never duplicates or extends the token. Field projection through (a borrow of) a live holder yields a statement-scoped borrow consumed by the receiving call; this is the sanctioned access path the loan protects. Scope-end consumption is the token's compiler-derived drop and counts as its auto-consume (LOAN-4).

LOAN-1 (Loan state). The checker attaches to every live binding a lexical loan set: a finite set of loans (k, h) with k in {uniq, shr} and h the binding currently holding the token. Loan sets change only at: issue (LOAN-2 adds), consume/reissue (LOAN-4 removes or replaces), holder transfer (LOAN-5 renames h), and holder scope end (removes, via auto-consume). No other statement form may alter any loan set, and per-binding loan sets plus affine liveness are the checker's only mutable state.

LOAN-2 (Issue). An operation declared or derived to `issue K` on a receiver or reference argument C requires at that statement: loans(C) = {} if kind(K) = uniq; no uniq loan in loans(C) if kind(K) = shr. The result must be let-bound to a fresh binding h of confined type K instantiated with C's region and brand, and (kind(K), h) is added to loans(C) on statement exit. Constructing a confined value that captures minted borrows is likewise an issue on each borrowed source binding — kind shr for `&`, uniq for `&uniq` — with the constructed binding as holder, under the same preconditions.

LOAN-3 (Freeze). While loans(C) is nonempty, C cannot be moved, rebound, assigned to, or passed by `own`. While loans(C) contains a uniq loan, no operation may be applied to C and no borrow may be minted from C, except within a statement that consumes or reissues that loan's holder (LOAN-4). While loans(C) contains only shr loans: minting `&C` and operations with `&` receiver mode are permitted, further shr issues are permitted, and minting `&uniq C` or applying any operation with `&uniq` or `own` receiver mode is rejected.

LOAN-4 (Consume, reissue, rebind). An op declared `consumes` receives a holder h by `own`; at that statement h's loan is removed from its issuer's loan set and h is dead. An op declared `reissues` consumes h and issues a replacement token on the same issuer with the same brand; the result must be let-bound fresh, or bound with `rebind h = e`, which requires identical confined type and brand and keeps h as the loan's holder (equivalently, `let h2 = e; rebind h = move h2` renames the holder back to h per LOAN-5). Within a consuming or reissuing statement, borrows of the issuer that the op's signature declares may be minted despite LOAN-3, because the loan is surrendered at that very operation. Scope end of a live holder is an implicit consume: the token's drop performs the form's declared release (e.g. unlock, cursor close).

LOAN-5 (Holder transfer and destructuring). Moving a live holder h consumes h and renames its loan's holder: moved into a confined constructor field, the holder becomes the constructed binding; moved by `own` into a call, the effect is read off the callee's signature per LOAN-6 (consume or reissue) — the callee's copy cannot survive the call by CONF-2 and LOAN-8, so removing the loan at the call statement is sound. A confined value may be destructured by match only while it holds no loan; a loan-holding token can end only via declared consume/reissue operations or scope end, so its interior borrows are never extracted into bare bindings.

LOAN-6 (Derived effects for non-form functions). A function's loan effects are derived from its signature alone, never from its body: (a) every `own` parameter of confined type has its loan consumed at the call; (b) every confined result must be let/rebind-bound and must carry (region, brand) parameters matching either an own confined parameter from (a) — then the call is a reissue on that token's issuer, with the bound result as new holder — or, failing that, exactly one form-typed reference parameter — then the call is a fresh issue on that argument binding, with LOAN-2's preconditions checked at the call; a confined result matching neither, or more than one candidate issuer, is rejected. (c) The body is checked under this same judgment with parameters as local bindings; LOAN-8 at its returns is what makes the derived caller-side effect sound. The caller trusts only the signature.

LOAN-7 (Generative instance brands). Every loanable form type carries a phantom brand; the brand of a value is its current owning binding, minted fresh at each `let` that binds a form value by own. Borrows and issued tokens carry the brand of the binding they were created from. Operations and functions unify brand parameters across their arguments and results; a brand mismatch, or one brand parameter instantiated by two distinct brands in a single call, is rejected. Because a binding with live loans cannot be moved (LOAN-3), no live token's brand is ever stale, so brand equality soundly identifies the issuing instance.

LOAN-8 (Region escape). A confined value may be returned only if every region parameter of its type is a region parameter of the enclosing function; returning a confined value whose region is introduced by any local scope of that function is rejected. Since regions are lexical and a holder's region is its issuer's region, every holder's scope lies within its issuer's scope, and holders are auto-consumed no later than issuer scope end, strictly before the issuer's compiler-derived drop (STOR-3 order follows region nesting).

LOAN-9 (Match agreement). Each match arm is checked from a copy of the pre-match state. Arms that fall through to the join must end with identical loan sets for all bindings live after the match and identical affine liveness for all bindings that survive the match; loan identity includes the holder name (after any rebind). Arms that exit via break or return instead contribute their state to the corresponding exit join, where the same pairwise agreement is required. Any disagreement rejects the match; the checker never merges states by approximation.

LOAN-10 (Loops). `loop { body }` is checked in one pass over the body starting from the loop-entry state. At the back edge, the loan set and holder liveness of every binding declared outside the loop must equal the loop-entry state exactly; all break states must agree pairwise per LOAN-9 and become the post-loop state. Tokens issued inside the body die at body end each iteration (auto-consume). Any cross-iteration change to an outside binding's loan state or holder liveness is rejected; consume-and-reissue with `rebind` is the sanctioned way to carry a loan across iterations.

LOAN-11 (Scoped par boundary). `par` bodies are named functions receiving only declared arguments; the par form's signature marks each argument slot replicate-shared or split-unique. Replicate-shared slots accept `&`-mode borrows or borrows of shr-backed confined values, which the trusted runtime may duplicate across bodies; split-unique slots accept a `&uniq`-mode buffer the trusted form divides into disjoint views, one per body, for the extent of the call. All minted borrows are consumed by the par statement (T-A) and the call returns only after join, so every captured region encloses the join lexically. Bodies' effect rows must match their slot modes (no writes through replicated arguments; writes only into a body's own split view). A token holding a uniq loan cannot be passed to a replicate-shared slot; such a statement is rejected.

LOAN-12 (Fail-closed form table). Every operation of a loanable sealed form carries a loan clause (`issues K`, `consumes`, `reissues`, or `none`), and an operation may be declared with `&` receiver mode only if the form's internals preserve all interior addresses under it — a sealed-form proof obligation subject to hostile fact-channel review before shipping. Every confined type names its kind and its issuing forms. Any call whose loan behavior is neither declared in the form table nor uniquely derived by LOAN-6 is rejected: absence of declaration is rejection, never default-accept.

derivations.derivation: 1. `let s = seq::new()`: LOAN-7 mints brand bs; loans(s)={}. 2. `s.push(own a)`: form table declares push `none` with `&uniq` receiver (LOAN-12); statement mints a fresh `&uniq s` (existing owner statement-scoped minting), consumed by the call (T-A); LOAN-3 precondition: loans(s)={} so `&uniq` minting is permitted; a is consumed (affine move); loan sets unchanged. 3. `s.push(own b)`: identical — minting is per statement, so a fresh `&uniq s` is minted again; loans(s) still {}. 4. Scope end: no holders live; s dropped per STOR-3. The loan judgment is vacuous on unloaned bindings, so it composes with statement-scoped minting without interference.

1. `let t = table::new()`: loans(t)={}, brand bt. 2. `let e = t.entry(k)`: entry declared `issues Entry['rt, bt]`, kind uniq from Entry's declaration (CONF-1); LOAN-2 precondition loans(t)={} holds; result let-bound fresh; exit state loans(t)={(uniq,e)}. 3. `t.insert(k2, v2)`: insert has `&uniq` receiver and is not a consume/reissue of holder e; LOAN-3 (uniq clause): while a uniq loan is live, no operation may be applied to t and no borrow may be minted from t except within a statement consuming e — REJECT at this statement. Rationale: insert may rehash and dangle e's interior pointer. 4. (unreached) `e.fill(v)` would have consumed the loan and re-opened t.

1. loans(t)={}. 2. `let c = t.range(lo,hi)`: issues Cursor, kind shr; LOAN-2 precondition (no uniq loan) holds; loans(t)={(shr,c)}. 3. Loop entry state: loans(t)={(shr,c)}, c live. 4. `let n = c.next()`: mints `&uniq c` from the confined binding (CONF-3), consumed by the call; next's clause is `none` for t (interior advance is trusted form internals under the shr loan); state unchanged. 5. Match, Some arm: `t.remove(k)` has `&uniq` receiver; LOAN-3 (shr clause): while only shr loans are live, `&uniq`-receiver operations on t are rejected — REJECT at this statement. Rationale: remove may delete or move the node the cursor rests on. 6. (For completeness, the None arm's break state loans(t)={(shr,c)} would have been the legal loop exit; the Some arm never reaches the join.)

1. `let m = mutex::new(own x)`: loans(m)={}, brand bm. 2. `let g = m.lock()`: lock issues Guard['rm,bm], kind uniq; LOAN-2 precondition loans(m)={} holds (`&m` receiver minted at this statement is fine, T-A consumes it); loans(m)={(uniq,g)}. 3. Loop entry state E: loans(m)={(uniq,g)}, g live. 4. `let p = predicate(&g)`: CONF-3 mints `&g` from the holder, consumed by the call; predicate has no confined result so LOAN-6 derives clause none; state unchanged. 5. Done arm: `break` records exit state = E. 6. Wait arm: `let g2 = cv.wait(&m, own g)`: wait declared `reissues`; brand unification bm(from &m) = bm(from g) succeeds (LOAN-7); g consumed, replacement issued on the same issuer m with holder g2 (LOAN-4); the `&m` mint inside this statement is permitted by LOAN-4's carve-out because the statement consumes m's loan holder; loans(m)={(uniq,g2)}. `rebind g = move g2`: LOAN-4/LOAN-5 holder rename into the dead same-typed slot g; loans(m)={(uniq,g)}, g live. Arm exit = E. 7. Back edge: state = E = entry (LOAN-10 satisfied); break states agree pairwise (one break). Post-loop state = E. 8. Scope end: g auto-consumed (LOAN-4: drop performs unlock), loans(m)={}; then cv and m dropped in region order (LOAN-8/STOR-3).

1. `let a = ring::new()`: brand ba, loans(a)={}. 2. `let b = ring::new()`: distinct statement, distinct brand bb (LOAN-7 generativity: one fresh brand per owning let). 3. `let pt = a.producer()`: issues Prod['ra,ba], kind shr; loans(a)={(shr,pt)}. 4. `let ct = b.consumer()`: issues Cons['rb,bb]; loans(b)={(shr,ct)}. 5. `feed(&b, &uniq pt, v)`: LOAN-7 unification of feed's single brand parameter b: from argument 1 (`&b`) it must equal bb; from argument 2 (pt's type Prod['ra, ba]) it must equal ba; ba != bb, so one brand parameter is instantiated by two distinct brands — REJECT at this statement. The symmetric swap (`feed(&a, &uniq ct, v)`) fails identically, and no op exists whose signature accepts a token without unifying its brand with the receiver's (LOAN-12 fail-closed), so cross-instance use is unrepresentable.

1. loans(t)={}, loans(data)={}. 2. `let env = Env { lut: &t }`: confined construction capturing a minted `&t` is an issue (LOAN-2 construction clause), kind shr from the `&` mode; precondition (no uniq loan on t) holds; loans(t)={(shr,env)}; env is a live holder. 3. `par::for_chunks(&uniq data, &env, body)`: slot 1 is split-unique — mints `&uniq data`; LOAN-3 check: loans(data)={} so `&uniq` minting permitted; the mint is consumed by the statement (T-A), and the trusted form divides data into disjoint per-body views for the call's extent (LOAN-11). Slot 2 is replicate-shared — mints `&env` from the holder (CONF-3); env is shr-backed, so replication across bodies is permitted (LOAN-11). Body effect rows check: writes('c) only through its own split view, reads('t) through the replicated shared borrow — matches slot modes. Regions: 't (of t), env's region, and data's region all lexically enclose this statement, and the call returns only after join, so every captured region encloses the join. State after: loans(t)={(shr,env)}, loans(data)={}. 4. `let v = t.get(k)`: `&` receiver op under a shr-only loan set — permitted (LOAN-3). 5. Scope end: env auto-consumed, loans(t)={}; data, t dropped after their holders per LOAN-8 ordering.

soundness_sketch: Claim: in any accepted program, no live token's interior access can be invalidated — the issuer object cannot be mutated (uniq case), uniquely mutated (shr case), moved, reallocated, or dropped while the token lives, on one thread or across the scoped-par boundary. The argument is an invariant preserved by every statement form. Invariant I: at every program point, the set of live values carrying interior access into the object owned by binding C is exactly the holders named in loans(C), with kinds at least as strong as their access. I holds initially (no confined values). It is preserved because confined types are the ONLY aggregates that may carry borrows (CONF-1), and confined values cannot enter heap data, sealed forms, non-confined aggregates, or globals (CONF-2), cannot be duplicated (CONF-3 affinity; minted borrows of holders are statement-scoped and consumed by T-A), and cannot outlive the region of their issuer (LOAN-8, regions lexical). So a token exists only as a stack binding inside its issuer's scope, and every event that creates, transfers, or destroys one is a distinct statement form the checker sees: issue ops and borrow-capturing constructions add the loan (LOAN-2), consume/reissue ops and scope-end drops remove or replace it (LOAN-4), moves rename the holder (LOAN-5). The one subtle case is own-passing a holder to a callee: the loan is removed at the call statement while the callee still runs, but the caller is suspended for the call's whole extent and the callee cannot persist the token (CONF-2) or return it except as a declared reissue (LOAN-6/LOAN-8), so when control returns, the token is dead and I holds again. Given I, LOAN-3 delivers the safety property directly: while any loan is live, C cannot be moved, rebound, or own-consumed (address stability and no early drop); while a uniq loan is live, no operation runs on C at all except loan-surrendering ones (no rehash, remove, push, or unlock under an entry token or guard); while only shr loans are live, only `&`-receiver operations run, and LOAN-12 makes non-invalidation of interior addresses a declaration-time proof obligation for every `&`-receiver op on a loanable form. Drop order is region order (LOAN-8/STOR-3), so a holder's auto-consume always precedes its issuer's drop. Brand generativity (LOAN-7) ensures a token is only ever surrendered to, or interpreted by, the instance that issued it — sound because brands are per-owning-binding and LOAN-3 forbids moving a loaned binding, so a live token's brand cannot be reseated. Match and loop agreement (LOAN-9/10) make loans(C) a well-defined function of the program point without dataflow: any two paths that could disagree are rejected, so the single-pass state IS the runtime truth on every accepted path. Across the scoped-par boundary: bodies receive only replicated shared access (backed by shr loans/borrows whose lexical regions enclose the join, with writes forbidden by effect rows) or form-guaranteed disjoint unique views of a buffer whose `&uniq` mint is consumed by the par statement itself — so during the call no caller alias exists, no body can reach another body's view, and no uniq-loaned token can be replicated (LOAN-11). Hence no data race and no invalidation is expressible in an accepted program; everything else is the sealed forms' internal proof burden, named and reviewed per LOAN-12.

adversarial_corpus.name: C1 escape-by-return beyond region
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-8: e's region parameter is the local region of t, not a region parameter of leak; the return statement is rejected. This is the load-bearing anti-dangle rule — the issuer dies at scope end, so the token must not cross the frame.

adversarial_corpus.name: C2 token stored in heap / non-confined struct
adversarial_corpus.why: CONF-1 rejects the non-confined struct declaration containing a confined field; CONF-2 rejects the box_new statement (confined value into heap data). Both channels for hiding a token beyond checker sight are closed at the declaring/storing statement.

adversarial_corpus.name: C3 two unique tokens from one binding
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-2: a uniq issue requires loans(t)={}; the second entry finds {(uniq,e1)} — and LOAN-3 already forbids any op on t. Two live entry tokens could both be dangled by one fill-induced rehash, so this must die.

adversarial_corpus.name: C4 shared-then-unique upgrade
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-2: uniq issue requires an empty loan set; a shr loan is live. An entry token permits fill (mutation/rehash) which would invalidate the live cursor; no upgrade path exists, matching OWN-5's exclusivity shape.

adversarial_corpus.name: C5 multiple shared cursors coexist
adversarial_corpus.why: Expressivity check: LOAN-2's shr precondition is only 'no uniq loan', LOAN-3's shr clause permits `&`-receiver ops and further shr issues. Sound because every op that could invalidate interiors requires &uniq/own receiver mode and is frozen out.

adversarial_corpus.name: C6 move while loaned
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-3 first clause: a binding with any live loan cannot be moved. The move would relocate/rebind the owner while c holds interior access, and would also invalidate brand identity (LOAN-7 relies on this rejection).

adversarial_corpus.name: C7 arm-imbalanced consume in match
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-9: fall-through arms disagree on both loans(t) and e's liveness; the match is rejected (reject-when-unsure). Accepting would make the post-match state path-dependent, which the one-pass judgment cannot represent; the blessed fix is consuming e in both arms.

adversarial_corpus.name: C8 helper-function issue (derived effect)
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-6(b): the confined result Entry['r,b] matches exactly one form-typed reference parameter, so the call site derives `issues` on tt with LOAN-2 checked there (loans(tt)={} holds); the wrapped-writer idiom works from the signature alone, which is the 'at least one blessed writing' requirement. Early consume then reuse composes.

adversarial_corpus.name: C9 loop-carried token used inside loop
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-10: no outside binding's loan state changes across the body (get is `&`-receiver, permitted by LOAN-3 shr clause; minting from c is per-statement). Back edge equals entry; the single break's state flows out. The common read-under-cursor idiom typechecks.

adversarial_corpus.name: C10 loop reissue without rebind (HARD CALL)
adversarial_corpus.why: Hard because every single statement is locally legal; the bug (iteration 2 runs unlocked and reads dead g) only exists across the back edge. LOAN-10's exact equality of loan sets AND holder liveness at the back edge catches it in one pass — this program is why the loop rule must compare holder liveness, not just loan sets.

adversarial_corpus.name: C11 early consume, then fresh issue and mixed reads
adversarial_corpus.why: Expressivity check for the consume-then-re-open rhythm: LOAN-4 removes loans at explicit consumes and at inner-scope auto-consume (e3's drop discharges the loan without an explicit fill... its drop performs the form's declared release), so sequential unique sessions and a following shared session all typecheck.

adversarial_corpus.name: C12 token wrapped into another confined value, then helper consume (HARD CALL)
adversarial_corpus.why: Hard because two mechanisms compose: constructor holder-transfer (LOAN-5) then signature-derived consume (LOAN-6a). Soundness hangs on the loan following the wrapper (no duplication: e is consumed by the construction) and on finish being unable to persist inner (CONF-2/LOAN-8 inside the callee). If the commented get were present it must reject via LOAN-3 — the transfer must not weaken the freeze.

adversarial_corpus.name: C13 brand confusion through a generic relock helper (HARD CALL)
adversarial_corpus.why: Hard: the signature is the legitimate reissue shape (C8's dual), and per LOAN-6(b) the result matches the own param, so the effect targets g1's real issuer m1 — the danger is the stray &m2. LOAN-7 unification kills it: relock's single brand parameter would need b2 (from &m2) and b1 (from g1) simultaneously. Also independently rejected because minting &m2... m2 is unloaned, so ONLY the brand check stands between this and unlocking m1 while holding m2's metadata — brands are load-bearing, not decorative.

adversarial_corpus.name: C14 uniq-loaned token into a par replicate slot
adversarial_corpus.why: LOAN-11 final clause: a token holding a uniq loan cannot be passed to a replicate-shared slot — duplicating unique interior access across bodies would be a race by construction. Minting &g is otherwise legal (CONF-3), so the par rule itself must carry this check; this probes the seam between CONF-3 minting and the par boundary.

decidability: The checker makes one syntax-directed pass over each function in ANF statement order carrying only: (i) the affine liveness of each binding in scope, (ii) each binding's loan set (pairs of kind and holder name, bounded by the number of bindings in scope), and (iii) the brand of each form-typed binding (the binding occurrence itself, so it is free). Every statement form has exactly one applicable rule chosen by syntax plus the callee's declared or derived signature clause — form-table lookup for sealed ops, the LOAN-6 positional derivation for helpers, and LOAN-12 rejects anything not uniquely covered — so there is never a choice point and hence no search: preconditions are membership/emptiness tests on the current loan sets evaluated on statement entry, effects are set insert/remove/rename applied on statement exit. Match arms are each checked once from a copy of the entry state and their exit states compared for equality (LOAN-9); `loop` bodies are checked once and the back-edge state compared for equality with the entry state (LOAN-10) — equality demanded up front replaces fixpoint iteration, because any body whose one-pass exit state differs from its entry state is rejected rather than iterated, so a single pass is both necessary and sufficient. Helper effects come from signatures alone (LOAN-6), making checking modular per function with no interprocedural analysis, and region/brand checks are unifications over finitely many parameters at each call. Total cost is O(program size x scope width), with termination immediate from the single traversal.
honest_risks: Rule-count honesty: I count 15 rules, but LOAN-6 packs three clauses and LOAN-4 packs consume/reissue/rebind/carve-out into single paragraphs; a hostile reviewer counting at strict OWN-1..13 granularity could call this 17-19 rules. Still under the 25 hard fail, but over the 15 target on an adversarial count.

The uniq-loan total freeze (LOAN-3) is deliberately brutal: while an entry token or mutex guard is live, even pure reads (t.get, m.is_poisoned) and passing &C to any helper are rejected. This blocks some legitimate idioms (e.g., logging table stats while holding an entry; a helper that takes &mutex for metadata while a guard is live elsewhere in the same function) and statically forbids single-thread re-lock (a feature, but also a restriction if a trusted try_lock idiom is wanted). The blessed-pattern doctrine can route around it, but the expressivity cost on real code is unmeasured.

Consume-on-own-pass removes the loan at the call statement while the callee still holds interior access for the call's duration. This is sound only because the caller is suspended and CONF-2/LOAN-8 prevent the token surviving the callee. Any future feature that lets caller state be observed during a call (async suspension points, signal handlers, re-entrant callbacks through form internals) silently breaks invariant I here. This interaction must be re-proved if the kernel ever grows such a feature.

LOAN-12's non-invalidation obligation for `&`-receiver ops on loanable forms is a trust axiom on sealed-form internals, not something the judgment checks: a form author declaring a shared op that reallocates (e.g., a lazily-rehashing read) would be unsound with no checker signal. It inherits the fact-channel hostile-review discipline, but it is the single largest trusted surface this design adds.

The loop rule (LOAN-10 exact back-edge equality plus rebind) rejects legitimately state-changing loops: a token consumed by a terminal op mid-loop with the loop continuing tokenless, tokens issued in iteration 1 and kept, or conditional reissue where only some arms rebind. Real cursor code (SQLite step loops with early close, retry loops that drop and re-acquire locks under backoff) may need contorted break-and-reloop shapes; whether this is acceptable verbosity (D2) or a fatal expressivity gap needs corpus evidence from the fourteen frozen operations.

Brand-as-owning-binding is only sound because LOAN-3 forbids moves while loaned; it also means brands do not survive ANY move, so an unloaned container moved into a new binding gets a fresh brand and previously mentioned brand parameters in surrounding generic code cannot refer to it — helpers that take a container by own and return it re-branded may not compose with sibling-token code. I believe every such program is one the design should reject anyway, but I have not enumerated the composition space; a paper adversarial pass on brand flow through own-parameters is needed.

CONF-3 extends statement-scoped minting (an owner privilege under theorem T-A) to confined holders. A callee receiving &uniq of two sibling shr tokens could swap their interior borrow fields; both interiors remain valid for the same issuer, and uniq tokens have no live siblings (LOAN-2), so I argue no invalidation is reachable — but T-A's proof was for owners, and this extension needs its own machine-checked case before the mechanism can be trusted at PROOF tier.

Match-arm exact agreement including holder names forces code duplication or dummy consumes in both arms for divergent-session code (C7's fix). Combined with reject-when-unsure at declaration for LOAN-6 ambiguity, some natural multi-container helpers (two form reference parameters plus one confined result with coincidental region overlap) are unwritable without extra brand/region annotations. These are the places writer-model friction will concentrate.
